blocks:
Ayahs

وَيْلٌ لِّلْمُطَفِّفِينَ (١)
Woe to the defrauders, who use short measures, (1)
ٱلَّذِينَ إِذَا ٱكْتَالُوا۟ عَلَى ٱلنَّاسِ يَسْتَوْفُونَ (٢)
who, when they measure [a commodity bought] from the people, take the full measure, (2)
وَإِذَا كَالُوهُمْ أَو وَّزَنُوهُمْ يُخْسِرُونَ (٣)
but diminish when they measure or weigh for them. (3)
أَلَا يَظُنُّ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَنَّهُم مَّبْعُوثُونَ (٤)
Do they not know that they will be resurrected (4)
لِيَوْمٍ عَظِيمٍ (٥)
on a tremendous day, (5)
يَوْمَ يَقُومُ ٱلنَّاسُ لِرَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٦)
a day when mankind will stand before the Lord of all the worlds? (6)
كَلَّآ إِنَّ كِتَـٰبَ ٱلْفُجَّارِ لَفِى سِجِّينٍ (٧)
Indeed, the record of the vicious is in Sijjeen. (7)
وَمَآ أَدْرَىٰكَ مَا سِجِّينٌ (٨)
And what will show you what is Sijjeen? (8)
كِتَـٰبٌ مَّرْقُومٌ (٩)
It is a written record. (9)
وَيْلٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِّلْمُكَذِّبِينَ (١٠)
Woe to the deniers on that day, (10)
ٱلَّذِينَ يُكَذِّبُونَ بِيَوْمِ ٱلدِّينِ (١١)
who deny the Day of Retribution; (11)
وَمَا يُكَذِّبُ بِهِۦٓ إِلَّا كُلُّ مُعْتَدٍ أَثِيمٍ (١٢)
and none denies it except every sinful transgressor. (12)
إِذَا تُتْلَىٰ عَلَيْهِ ءَايَـٰتُنَا قَالَ أَسَـٰطِيرُ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ (١٣)
When Our signs are recited to him, he says, ‘Myths of the ancients!’ (13)
كَلَّا ۖ بَلْ ۜ رَانَ عَلَىٰ قُلُوبِهِم مَّا كَانُوا۟ يَكْسِبُونَ (١٤)
No, that is not the case! Rather, their hearts have been sullied by what they have been earning. (14)
كَلَّآ إِنَّهُمْ عَن رَّبِّهِمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ لَّمَحْجُوبُونَ (١٥)
Indeed, they will be alienated from their Lord on that day. (15)
ثُمَّ إِنَّهُمْ لَصَالُوا۟ ٱلْجَحِيمِ (١٦)
Afterward they will enter hell, (16)
ثُمَّ يُقَالُ هَـٰذَا ٱلَّذِى كُنتُم بِهِۦ تُكَذِّبُونَ (١٧)
then told, ‘This is what you used to deny!’ (17)
كَلَّآ إِنَّ كِتَـٰبَ ٱلْأَبْرَارِ لَفِى عِلِّيِّينَ (١٨)
Indeed, the record of the pious is in Illeeyun. (18)
وَمَآ أَدْرَىٰكَ مَا عِلِّيُّونَ (١٩)
And what will show you what is Illeeyun? (19)
كِتَـٰبٌ مَّرْقُومٌ (٢٠)
It is a written record, (20)
يَشْهَدُهُ ٱلْمُقَرَّبُونَ (٢١)
witnessed by those brought near [to Allah]. (21)

(Commentary)

The Surah opens with a threat against those who defraud in measure and weight, warning them that they shall be resurrected for recompense on a Great Day, which is the Day of Resurrection. It then transitions to detail what will occur on that day to the profligates and the pious.

Considering the context, it is most appropriate that the beginning of the Surah, which contains the threat against the defrauders, was revealed in Medina. As for the verses that follow until the end of the Surah, they are susceptible to being applied to both Meccan and Medinan contexts.

His saying, the Exalted: وَيْلٌ لِلْمُطَفِّفِينَ — "Woe to the defrauders!" (83:1). This is an invocation against the defrauders (المطففين). Defrauding (التطفيف) is the act of giving short measure or weight. Allah, the Exalted, has forbidden it and termed it "corruption in the earth" (إفساد في الأرض), as seen in what He narrated of the speech of Shu‘ayb: وَيَا قَوْمِ أَوْفُوا الْمِكْيَالَ وَالْمِيزَانَ بِالْقِسْطِ وَلَا تَبْخَسُوا النَّاسَ أَشْيَاءَهُمْ وَلَا تَعْثَوْا فِي الْأَرْضِ مُفْسِدِينَ — "O my people! Give full measure and weight with justice, and do not cheat the people of their goods, and do not act wickedly on the earth, causing corruption" (11:85). The discussion regarding the meaning of it being "corruption in the earth" has already preceded in the commentary of that verse.

His saying, the Exalted: الَّذِينَ إِذَا اكْتَالُوا عَلَى النَّاسِ يَسْتَوْفُونَ وَإِذَا كَالُوهُمْ أَوْ وَزَنُوهُمْ يُخْسِرُونَ — "those who, when they take a measure from people, take it in full, but when they measure for them or weigh for them, they give less" (83:2-3). Taking a measure (الاكتيال) from people means receiving from them by measure. Its being made transitive with the preposition "upon" (على) is to convey the sense of harm [to the other party]. Measuring (الكيل) is giving to them by measure. It is said: "he measured for him his food" (كاله طعامه) and "he weighed it for him" (وزنه), or "he measured for him" (كال له) and "he weighed for him" (وزن له). The first is the dialect of the people of the Hijaz, and the Revelation followed it, while the second is the dialect of others, as mentioned in al-Majma‘. Taking in full (الاستيفاء) is to take one's right completely and perfectly. Giving less (الإخسار) is to cause someone to fall into loss (خسارة).

The meaning is: those who, when they take from people by measure, take their right completely and perfectly, but when they give to people by measure or weight, they diminish it, thereby causing them to fall into loss.

Thus, the content of both verses together constitutes a single condemnation: that they observe the right for themselves but do not observe it for others. In other words, they do not observe for others the same right they observe for themselves. In this lies the corruption of human society (الاجتماع الإنساني), which is built upon the equilibrium of reciprocal rights; in its corruption lies every form of evil.

Weighing (الاتزان) was not mentioned alongside taking a measure (الاكتيال), whereas weight was mentioned alongside measuring when He said: وَإِذَا كَالُوهُمْ أَوْ وَزَنُوهُمْ — "but when they measure for them or weigh for them." It has been said that this is because the defrauders were sellers who mostly bought large quantities of grains, legumes, and similar goods, then earned through them by selling them little by little, gradually. Their custom regarding many of these goods was to take and give by measure, not by weight; thus, mentioning only "taking a measure" (الاكتيال) in the verse is based on what was most common.

It has also been said that weighing was not mentioned because buying and selling occur through both measure and weight, so mentioning one points to the other. However, this is countered by the fact that what was mentioned regarding "taking a measure" applies to "giving a measure" as well, yet weight was mentioned with the latter. Thus, this reasoning is not free from arbitrariness.

It has also been said that the two verses depict the custom of those regarding whom the Surah was revealed, for they used to buy only by measure but sell by both measure and weight. This view is a claim without evidence.

There are other explanations mentioned for restricting the mention to "taking a measure" in the verse, but none of them are free from weakness.

His saying, the Exalted: أَلَا يَظُنُّ أُولَئِكَ أَنَّهُمْ مَبْعُوثُونَ لِيَوْمٍ عَظِيمٍ — "Do they not know that they will be resurrected for a tremendous day" (83:4-5). The interrogative is for denunciation and wonder. Thought (الظن) here carries its well-known meaning. The reference to the defrauders with "those" (أُولَئِكَ) — which is used for distant objects — is to indicate their distance from the mercy of Allah. The "Great Day" (اليوم العظيم) is the Day of Resurrection, in which they will be recompensed for their deeds.

Sufficing with the "thought" (ظن) or "supposition" of the Resurrection — despite the obligation of having certain knowledge (العلم) of the Return (المعاد) — is because the mere supposition of danger and harm in an act necessitates avoiding it and guarding against committing it, even if certain knowledge is absent. Thus, the thought of being resurrected for a Great Day in which Allah will hold people accountable for what they earned should, by its nature, deter them from committing this great sin that entails painful punishment.

It has also been said that "thought" (الظن) in the verse means "certain knowledge" (العلم).

His saying, the Exalted: يَوْمَ يَقُومُ النَّاسُ لِرَبِّ الْعَالَمِينَ — "the day when mankind will stand before the Lord of all the worlds" (83:6). What is meant is their rising from their graves — a metaphor for their being clothed in life after death — for His judgment and decree among them.

His saying, the Exalted: كَلَّا إِنَّ كِتَابَ الْفُجَّارِ لَفِي سِجِّينٍ وَمَا أَدْرَاكَ مَا سِجِّينٌ كِتَابٌ مَرْقُومٌ وَيْلٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِلْمُكَذِّبِينَ — "No indeed! The record of the vicious is surely in Sijjin. And what will show you what is Sijjin? It is a transcript inscribed. Woe to the deniers on that day" (83:7-10). This is a deterrent (ردع) — as has been said — from what they were upon of defrauding and heedlessness of the Resurrection and the Reckoning.

Regarding His saying: إِنَّ كِتَابَ الْفُجَّارِ لَفِي سِجِّينٍ — "The record of the vicious is surely in Sijjin," that which contemplation (التدبر) of the context of these four verses yields — by comparing them to one another and comparing the whole group to the group of His saying: كَلَّا إِنَّ كِتَابَ الْأَبْرارِ لَفِي عِلِّيِّينَ — "No indeed! The record of the pious is surely in ‘Illiyyun" (83:18) until the end of the four verses — is that what is meant by Sijjin (سجين) is the opposite of ‘Illiyyun (عليين). The latter means "height upon doubled height," so the former contains something of the meaning of "lowness" and "confinement" within it, as indicated by His saying: ثُمَّ رَدَدْنَاهُ أَسْفَلَ سَافِلِينَ — "then We relegated him to the lowest of the low" (95:5). Thus, it is most likely an intensive form derived from "prison" (السجن) meaning confinement (الحبس), like sikīr (drunkard) and sharīb (heavy drinker) from sukr (drunkenness) and shurb (drinking). Its meaning is that which imprisons whoever enters it eternally, as has been said.

The "Record" (الكتاب) here means "that which is written" (المكتوب), derived from "writing" (الكتابة) in the sense of a "decreed judgment" (القضاء المحتوم). What is meant by the "record of the profligates" (كتاب الفجار) is the recompense Allah has ordained for them and established by His decreed judgment.

The sum of the verse is that what Allah has established of their recompense or prepared for them is surely in Sijjin, which is a prison that confines whoever enters it for a long or eternal duration.

His saying: وَمَا أَدْرَاكَ مَا سِجِّينٌ — "And what will show you what is Sijjin?" is intended for intimidation (التهويل).

His saying: كِتَابٌ مَرْقُومٌ — "It is a transcript inscribed" is the predicate of a deleted subject, which is a pronoun referring back to Sijjin. The sentence is an explanation of Sijjin. "Record" (كتاب) here also means "that which is written," derived from "writing" in the sense of "decree" and "establishment." "Inscribed" (مرقوم) is from al-raqm (الرقم). Al-Raghib said: "al-raqm is thick writing; it is also said to be the dotting/vocalizing of a book." He continued: "His saying, the Exalted: كِتَابٌ مَرْقُومٌ has been interpreted in both ways." End quote. The second meaning is more appropriate for the context, as it would be an indication that what is written for them is distinct and clear with no ambiguity; i.e., the decree is certain and will not fail.

The conclusion is that Sijjin is a decree (مقضي) against them, established (مثبت) for them, clear and distinct with no ambiguity.

There is no harm in the "record" being a container for the "record" according to this meaning, because that is of the nature of the whole containing the part, which is unobjectionable. Thus, Sijjin would be a comprehensive record containing what has been decreed for the profligates and others who deserve punishment.

His saying: وَيْلٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِلْمُكَذِّبِينَ — "Woe to the deniers on that day" is a lament and an invocation against the profligates, and it contains their definition as "the deniers" (المكذبين). "On that day" (يَوْمَئِذٍ) is an adverbial qualifier for His saying: إِنَّ كِتَابَ الْفُجَّارِ لَفِي سِجِّينٍ according to the meaning; i.e., let the profligates — who are the deniers — perish on that day when what Allah has written for them and decreed against them of recompense is realized, and the punishment He prepared for them befalls them.

This is what contemplation of these four verses yields, as they possess a single, continuous context with harmonious parts.

The commentators have various scattered opinions regarding the vocabulary and sentences of these four verses. For instance, they say that "the record" (الكتاب) in His saying: إِنَّ كِتَابَ الْفُجَّارِ means "that which is written," referring to the scroll of their deeds (صحيفة أعمالهم). It has also been said it is a verbal noun meaning "writing," with a deleted possessive noun, the estimation being "the writing of the deeds of the profligates is in Sijjin."

They also say that "the profligates" (الفجار) is more general than "the deniers," thus including both disbelievers and the sinful (الفسقة) among the believers.

They also say that what is meant by Sijjin is the seventh, lowest earth where the record of the profligates is placed. It has been said it is a valley in Hell, or a pit therein. It has been said Sijjin is the name of their record. It has been said the first Sijjin is the name of the place where their record is placed, and the second is the name of their record itself. It has been said it is the name of a comprehensive record, a "register of evil" (ديوان الشر) in which the deeds of the profligates among the jinn and humans are recorded. It has been said it refers to loss and humiliation, similar to the saying: "So-and-so reached the abyss" (الحضيض) when he becomes utterly obscure. It has been said it is Sijjīl (سجيل), with its lām replaced by a nūn, just as Jibrīn is said for Jibrīl. These are among the various things said.

They also say that His saying: كِتَابٌ مَرْقُومٌ is not an explanation and interpretation of Sijjin, but rather an interpretation of the "record" mentioned in His saying: إِنَّ كِتَابَ الْفُجَّارِ.

They also say that His saying: وَيْلٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِلْمُكَذِّبِينَ is connected to His saying: يَوْمَ يَقُومُ النَّاسُ لِرَبِّ الْعَالَمِينَ, and the three verses occurring between the two are a parenthetical insertion.

If you reflect on these sayings, you will find many of them to be pure arbitrariness with no evidence. Furthermore, they sever the single, continuous context of the verses, which parallels the single, continuous context in the four upcoming verses describing the record of the pious. Therefore, we shall not prolong the discussion by addressing each one individually or critiquing them.

His saying, the Exalted: الَّذِينَ يُكَذِّبُونَ بِيَوْمِ الدِّينِ — "those who deny the Day of Retribution" (83:11). This is an interpretation of "the deniers" (المكذبين). The apparent meaning of the verse — supported by the subsequent verses — is that what is meant by "denial" (التكذيب) is explicit verbal denial. Thus, the condemnation is specific to the disbelievers and does not include the sinful among the people of faith; it would not include all defrauders, but only the disbelievers among them.

Unless, of course, "denial" is intended to encompass "practical denial" (التكذيب العملي), as might be supported by His previous saying: أَلَا يَظُنُّ أُولَئِكَ أَنَّهُمْ مَبْعُوثُونَ — "Do they not know that they will be resurrected." In that case, it would include the profligates among the believers just as it includes the disbelievers.

His saying, the Exalted: وَمَا يُكَذِّبُ بِهِ إِلَّا كُلُّ مُعْتَدٍ أَثِيمٍ — "and none denies it except every sinful transgressor" (83:12). The "transgressor" (المعتدي) is the active participle of "transgression" (الاعتداء) meaning to overstep. What is meant is one who oversteps the bounds of servitude (العبودية). The "sinful" (الأثيم) is one who commits many sins (الآثام) such that they accumulate upon one another due to his immersion in whims.

It is well known that the only deterrent that restrains one from disobedience is faith in the Resurrection and Recompense. One who is immersed in whims, whose heart is attached to transgression and sin, finds his soul refusing to submit to that which would restrain it or to abstain from sins. This eventually leads to the denial of the Resurrection and Recompense. Allah, the Exalted, said: ثُمَّ كَانَ عَاقِبَةَ الَّذِينَ أَسَاءُوا السُّوأَى أَنْ كَذَّبُوا بِآيَاتِ اللَّهِ وَكَانُوا بِهَا يَسْتَهْزِئُونَ — "Then the fate of those who committed misdeeds was that they denied the signs of Allah and they used to deride them" (30:10).

His saying, the Exalted: إِذَا تُتْلَى عَلَيْهِ آيَاتُنَا قَالَ أَسَاطِيرُ الْأَوَّلِينَ — "When Our signs are recited to him, he says, ‘Myths of the ancients!’" (83:13). What is meant by "signs" (الآيات) are the verses of the Quran, by the evidence of His saying "recited" (تُتْلَى). "Myths" (الأساطير) are what they have inscribed and written, meaning the falsehoods of past nations. The meaning is: when the verses of the Quran are recited to him — those which warn them against disobedience and threaten them with the Resurrection and Recompense — he says: "These are falsehoods."

His saying, the Exalted: كَلَّا بَلْ رَانَ عَلَى قُلُوبِهِمْ مَا كَانُوا يَكْسِبُونَ — "No indeed! Rather their hearts have been sullied by what they have been earning" (83:14). This is a deterrent against what the deniers said: "Myths of the ancients!" Al-Raghib said: "al-rayn (الرين) is a rust that covers a polished thing. Allah, the Exalted, said: بَلْ رَانَ عَلَى قُلُوبِهِمْ, meaning that became like rust upon the clarity of their hearts, so the knowledge of good from evil became obscured for them." End quote. Thus, the fact that what they were earning — which are sins — became rust upon their hearts means that sins acted as a barrier between them and perceiving the truth as it truly is.

From this verse, several points emerge:

First: That evil deeds leave imprints and images upon the soul (النفس) by which it becomes imprinted and shaped.

Second: That these imprints and images prevent the soul from perceiving the truth as it is and act as a barrier between the soul and the truth.

Third: That the soul, according to its primordial nature (الفطرة), possesses a purity and clarity by which it perceives the truth as it is, distinguishes between it and falsehood, and differentiates between godwariness (التقوى) and profligacy (الفجور). Allah, the Exalted, said: وَنَفْسٍ وَمَا سَوَّاهَا فَأَلْهَمَهَا فُجُورَهَا وَتَقْوَاهَا — "by the soul and Him who fashioned it, and inspired it with [the discernment of] its vices and its God-wariness" (91:7-8).

His saying, the Exalted: كَلَّا إِنَّهُمْ عَنْ رَبِّهِمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ لَمَحْجُوبُونَ — "No indeed! They will surely be barred from their Lord on that day" (83:15). This is a deterrent against earning sins that act as a barrier between the heart and the perception of truth. What is meant by their being "barred from their Lord" on the Day of Resurrection is their deprivation of the honor of proximity (القرب) and station (المنزلة). This is perhaps what was intended by those who said it means they are barred from the mercy of their Lord.

As for the lifting of the veil (الحجاب) in the sense of the falling away of intermediary causes (الأسباب المتوسطة) between Him, the Exalted, and His creation, and the complete knowledge of Him, the Exalted — that is attained by everyone. Allah, the Exalted, said: لِمَنِ الْمُلْكُ الْيَوْمَ لِلَّهِ الْوَاحِدِ الْقَهَّارِ — "‘To whom does the sovereignty belong today?’ ‘To Allah, the One, the All-paramount!’" (40:16), and He said: وَيَعْلَمُونَ أَنَّ اللَّهَ هُوَ الْحَقُّ الْمُبِينُ — "and they will know that Allah is the Manifest Truth" (24:25).

His saying, the Exalted: ثُمَّ إِنَّهُمْ لَصَالُو الْجَحِيمِ — "Then they will surely enter hell" (83:16). That is, they are entering it, remaining in it, or enduring its heat, according to how some have interpreted it. The word "then" (ثُمَّ) in this verse and the one after it indicates a delay in terms of the rank of the discourse.

His saying, the Exalted: ثُمَّ يُقَالُ هَذَا الَّذِي كُنْتُمْ بِهِ تُكَذِّبُونَ — "Then it will be said [to them], ‘This is what you used to deny!’" (83:17). This is a rebuke and a reprimand. The speaker is either the keepers of the Fire or the people of Paradise.

His saying, the Exalted: كَلَّا إِنَّ كِتَابَ الْأَبْرَارِ لَفِي عِلِّيِّينَ وَمَا أَدْرَاكَ مَا عِلِّيُّونَ كِتَابٌ مَرْقُومٌ — "No indeed! The record of the pious is surely in ‘Illiyyun. And what will show you what is ‘Illiyyun? It is a transcript inscribed" (83:18-20). This is a deterrent in the same sense as the deterrent in His saying: كَلَّا إِنَّ كِتَابَ الْفُجَّارِ. ‘Illiyyun (عليون) — as previously mentioned — is height upon doubled height. it applies to the high degrees and the stations of proximity to Allah, the Exalted, just as Sijjin is its opposite.

The discussion regarding the meaning of these three verses is analogous to the discussion regarding the three preceding verses that parallel them, starting from His saying: إِنَّ كِتَابَ الْفُجَّارِ لَفِي سِجِّينٍ وَمَا أَدْرَاكَ مَا سِجِّينٌ كِتَابٌ مَرْقُومٌ.

The meaning is that what has been written for the pious and decreed as a recompense for their piety is surely in ‘Illiyyun. And what will show you what is ‘Illiyyun? It is a matter written and decreed with a certain, necessary decree, clear and without ambiguity.

The commentators have opinions regarding these verses similar to their opinions on the previous verses. However, among their sayings regarding ‘Illiyyun is that it is the seventh heaven under the Throne, containing the souls of the believers. It has been said it is the Lote Tree of the Boundary (سدرة المنتهى) where deeds end. It has been said it is a tablet of emerald under the Throne, suspended, in which their deeds are written. It has been said they are high ranks surrounded by majesty. The discussion regarding these is the same as the discussion regarding their previous sayings.

His saying, the Exalted: يَشْهَدُهُ الْمُقَرَّبُونَ — "witnessed by those brought near [to Allah]" (83:21). What is most appropriate to the previously mentioned meaning of the verses is that "witnessing" (يَشْهَدُهُ) is from al-shuhūd in the sense of "beholding" (المعاينة). The "those brought near" (المقربون) are a group among the people of Paradise who are of a higher degree than the general body of the pious (الأبرار), as will be understood from His saying: عَيْنًا يَشْرَبُ بِهَا الْمُقَرَّبُونَ — "a spring where those brought near [to Allah] drink" (83:28). Thus, what is meant is their beholding it by Allah showing it to them. Allah, the Exalted, said regarding the like of this concerning the matter of Hell: كَلَّا لَوْ تَعْلَمُونَ عِلْمَ الْيَقِينِ لَتَرَوُنَّ الْجَحِيمَ — "No indeed! Were you to know with certain knowledge, you would surely see hell" (102:6-7). From this, it appears that "those brought near" are the people of certainty (أهل اليقين).

It has been said that "witnessing" (الشهادة) is "presence" (الحضور) and that "those brought near" are the angels. The meaning would be the presence of the angels over the scroll of their deeds when they ascend with it to Allah, the Glorified.

It has also been said that "those brought near" are the pious and the angels together.

Both these sayings are based on the premise that the "record" (الكتاب) means the scroll of deeds, the weakness of which has already been mentioned.

(Narrative Discussion)

In Tafsir al-Qummi, and in the narration of Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him), he said: "It was revealed — meaning Surah al-Mutaffifin — to the Prophet of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) when he arrived in Medina, and they were at that time the worst of people in measuring — so they improved their measuring."

In Usul al-Kafi, with its chain of narration to Abu Hamzah al-Thumali, he said: "I heard Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him) say: 'Indeed, Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, created us from the highest of ‘Illiyyun — and He created the hearts of our followers (شيعتنا) from that which He created us from — and He created their bodies from below that — so their hearts yearn for us because they were created from what we were created from.' Then he recited this verse: كَلَّا إِنَّ كِتَابَ الْأَبْرَارِ لَفِي عِلِّيِّينَ — وَمَا أَدْرَاكَ مَا عِلِّيُّونَ كِتَابٌ مَرْقُومٌ يَشْهَدُهُ الْمُقَرَّبُونَ."

"And He created the hearts of our enemies from Sijjin — and He created the hearts of their followers from what He created them from — and their bodies from below that. Their hearts yearn for them because they were created from what they were created from." Then he recited this verse: كَلَّا إِنَّ كِتَابَ الْفُجَّارِ لَفِي سِجِّينٍ — وَمَا أَدْرَاكَ مَا سِجِّينٌ كِتَابٌ مَرْقُومٌ — وَيْلٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِلْمُكَذِّبِينَ."

I say: A similar narration is recorded in Usul al-Kafi through another path from al-Thumali from him (peace be upon him). It was also narrated in ‘Ilal al-Shara’i‘ with a chain containing an attribution (رفع) to Zayd al-Shahham from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) similarly. These traditions — as you see — support what we have presented regarding the meaning of the verses.

In Tafsir al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: كَلَّا إِنَّ كِتَابَ الْفُجَّارِ لَفِي سِجِّينٍ, he said: "What Allah has written for them of punishment is surely in Sijjin."

In the same source, in the narration of Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him), he said: "Sijjin is the seventh earth and ‘Illiyyun is the seventh heaven."

I say: This narration, if authentic, is based on the attribution of Paradise and Hell to the directions of height and lowness by way of a certain figurative association (عناية). There are parallels to this in the narrations, such as counting the grave as a garden from the gardens of Paradise or a pit from the pits of Hell, and counting the Valley of Barhut as a place for Hell.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn al-Mubarak extracted from Sa‘id ibn al-Musayyib who said: "Salman and ‘Abdullah ibn Salam met, and one of them said to his companion: 'If you die before me, then meet me and inform me of what your Lord did with you; and if I die before you, I shall meet you and inform you.' ‘Abdullah said: 'How can this be?' He replied: 'Yes, indeed the souls of the believers are in an intermediate realm (برزخ) of the earth, going wherever they wish, while the soul of the disbeliever is in Sijjin.' And Allah knows best."

In Usul al-Kafi, with its chain of narration to Zurarah from Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him), he said: "There is no servant except that in his heart there is a white spot. When he commits a sin, a black spot emerges in that white spot. If he repents, that blackness goes away. But if he persists in sins, that blackness increases until it covers the whiteness. When the whiteness is covered, its owner will never return to good. And that is the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: كَلَّا بَلْ رَانَ عَلَى قُلُوبِهِمْ مَا كَانُوا يَكْسِبُونَ."

I say: This meaning is also narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from several compilers of traditions from Abu Hurayrah from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم).

In the same source, with its chain of narration to ‘Abdullah ibn Muhammad al-Hajjal from some of our companions in an attributed (رفع) report, he said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'Remind one another, meet one another, and converse, for conversation is the polish of the hearts — indeed, hearts rust (ترين) just as the sword rusts, and its polish is conversation...'" (the tradition).

From Rawdat al-Wa‘izin, al-Baqir (peace be upon him) said: "Nothing is more corruptive to the heart than a sin. Indeed, the heart commits a sin and it continues to affect it until it overcomes it, making its bottom its top and its top its bottom."

The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "When the believer sins, a black spot appears in his heart. If he repents, desists, and seeks forgiveness, his heart is polished of it. But if he increases [in sin], it increases. That is the rust (الران) which Allah, the Exalted, mentioned in His Book: كَلَّا بَلْ رَانَ عَلَى قُلُوبِهِمْ مَا كَانُوا يَكْسِبُونَ."

Ayahs

إِنَّ ٱلْأَبْرَارَ لَفِى نَعِيمٍ (٢٢)
The pious shall be amid bliss, (22)
عَلَى ٱلْأَرَآئِكِ يَنظُرُونَ (٢٣)
observing, [as they recline] on couches. (23)
تَعْرِفُ فِى وُجُوهِهِمْ نَضْرَةَ ٱلنَّعِيمِ (٢٤)
You will perceive in their faces the freshness of bliss. (24)
يُسْقَوْنَ مِن رَّحِيقٍ مَّخْتُومٍ (٢٥)
They will be served with a sealed pure wine, (25)
خِتَـٰمُهُۥ مِسْكٌ ۚ وَفِى ذَٰلِكَ فَلْيَتَنَافَسِ ٱلْمُتَنَـٰفِسُونَ (٢٦)
whose seal is musk—for such let the viers vie— (26)
وَمِزَاجُهُۥ مِن تَسْنِيمٍ (٢٧)
and whose seasoning is from Tasneem, (27)
عَيْنًا يَشْرَبُ بِهَا ٱلْمُقَرَّبُونَ (٢٨)
a spring where those brought near [to Allah] drink. (28)
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ أَجْرَمُوا۟ كَانُوا۟ مِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ يَضْحَكُونَ (٢٩)
Indeed the guilty used to laugh at the faithful, (29)
وَإِذَا مَرُّوا۟ بِهِمْ يَتَغَامَزُونَ (٣٠)
and when they passed them by they would wink at each other, (30)
وَإِذَا ٱنقَلَبُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰٓ أَهْلِهِمُ ٱنقَلَبُوا۟ فَكِهِينَ (٣١)
and when they returned to their folks they would return amused, (31)
وَإِذَا رَأَوْهُمْ قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ لَضَآلُّونَ (٣٢)
and when they saw them they would say, ‘Indeed those are the astray!’ (32)
وَمَآ أُرْسِلُوا۟ عَلَيْهِمْ حَـٰفِظِينَ (٣٣)
Though they were not sent to watch over them. (33)
فَٱلْيَوْمَ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ مِنَ ٱلْكُفَّارِ يَضْحَكُونَ (٣٤)
So today the faithful will laugh at the faithless, (34)
عَلَى ٱلْأَرَآئِكِ يَنظُرُونَ (٣٥)
observing from their couches: (35)
هَلْ ثُوِّبَ ٱلْكُفَّارُ مَا كَانُوا۟ يَفْعَلُونَ (٣٦)
Have the faithless been requited for what they used to do? (36)

(Commentary)

This is an exposition containing some detail regarding the sublime status of the righteous (الأبرار) and the greatness of their station with Allah, the Exalted, as well as the abundance of their life in Paradise. It further explains that despite the disbelievers mocking them, winking at one another in derision, and laughing at them, the righteous shall soon laugh at the disbelievers while observing the punishment that befalls them.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ الْأَبْرارَ لَفِي نَعِيمٍ "Indeed the pious shall be in bliss" (83:22). Bliss (النعيم) refers to abundant favor (النعمة الكثيرة). The use of the indefinite form (تنكير) indicates the magnificence of its status. The meaning is that the righteous are truly immersed in an abundance of favor that description cannot encompass.

His saying, the Exalted: عَلَى الْأَرائِكِ يَنْظُرُونَ "on couches, watching" (83:23). Couches (الأرائك) is the plural of arikah (أريكة), which generally refers to a raised bed, specifically one in a decorated chamber for a bride. The absolute use of His saying, يَنْظُرُونَ "watching", without restriction, supports the view that it refers to their looking at the delightful sights of Paradise and the everlasting bliss therein. It has been said that it refers to watching the punishment meted out to the disbelievers, but this is not the primary meaning.

His saying, the Exalted: تَعْرِفُ فِي وُجُوهِهِمْ نَضْرَةَ النَّعِيمِ "You will perceive in their faces the freshness of bliss" (83:24). Freshness (النضرة) refers to radiance, beauty, and luster. The address is to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) on the basis that he is one who would look and thus perceive; therefore, the ruling is general. The meaning is that anyone who looks at their faces will recognize the radiance of the bliss in which they dwell.

His saying, the Exalted: يُسْقَوْنَ مِنْ رَحِيقٍ مَخْتُومٍ "They will be given to drink a pure wine, sealed" (83:25). Pure wine (الرحيق) is a clear drink, free from any adulteration. This is complemented by its description as "sealed" (مختوم), for a seal is only placed upon something precious and pure to keep it safe from adulteration, mixing, or the introduction of anything that might spoil it.

His saying, the Exalted: خِتامُهُ مِسْكٌ وَفِي ذلِكَ فَلْيَتَنافَسِ الْمُتَنافِسُونَ "whose seal is musk—for that let the aspirers aspire" (83:26). It is said that "seal" (الختام) here means the substance used for sealing; i.e., that which it is sealed with is musk instead of the clay or similar materials used for sealing in this world. It is also said to mean that the final taste experienced by the drinker is the scent of musk.

His saying: وَفِي ذلِكَ فَلْيَتَنافَسِ الْمُتَنافِسُونَ "for that let the aspirers aspire". Aspiration (التنافس) — the act of striving to outdo others for something — implies the meaning of racing (التسابق) according to the context. The Exalted said: سابِقُوا إِلى مَغْفِرَةٍ مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ وَجَنَّةٍ "Take the lead towards forgiveness from your Lord and a paradise" (57:21), and He said: فَاسْتَبِقُوا الْخَيْراتِ "so excel one another in good works" (5:48). Thus, it is an encouragement toward the described "sealed pure wine."

A grammatical problem has been raised regarding the verse: that it involves a conjunction entering upon another conjunction, as the implied structure would be "so let them aspire in that..." etc. The response is that the speech assumes a conditional particle, and the fa (ف) occurs in its response (جواب), while the adverbial phrase (الظرف) is placed forward to serve as a substitute for the condition. The implied meaning is: "If aspiration is desired, then in that let the aspirers aspire."

It is also possible to say that His saying: وَفِي ذلِكَ "and in that" is conjoined to another omitted adverbial phrase related to His saying: فَلْيَتَنافَسِ "let [them] aspire", as indicated by the context. Since the discourse concerns the description of the bliss of Paradise, His saying وَفِي ذلِكَ provides an emphasized encouragement by specifying the ruling after generalizing it. The meaning would be: "Let the aspirers aspire in the bliss of Paradise generally, and in the sealed pure wine which they are given to drink specifically." This is like saying: "Honor the believers, and the righteous among them specifically," or "Do not be a fault-finder, and toward the scholars specifically."

His saying, the Exalted: وَمِزاجُهُ مِنْ تَسْنِيمٍ "and whose mixture is of Tasnim" (83:27). Mixture (المزاج) is that which is mixed into something. Tasnim (تسنيم), as explained by the following verse, is a spring in Paradise that Allah named Tasnim. In its linguistic root, there is the meaning of elevation and filling. It is said: sannamahu, meaning he elevated it. From this comes the hump (سنام) of a camel. It is also said: sannama al-ina', meaning he filled the vessel.

His saying, the Exalted: عَيْناً يَشْرَبُ بِهَا الْمُقَرَّبُونَ "a spring from which those brought near drink" (83:28). It is said "he drank it" (شربه) and "he drank with it" (شرب به) with the same meaning. "A spring" (عَيْناً) is in the accusative case for the purpose of praise (المدح) or specification (الاختصاص), and "from which those brought near drink" is its description. The whole phrase serves as an explanation of Tasnim.

The import of the verse is that those brought near (المقربون) — those who have attained the highest proximity to Allah — drink Tasnim pure and unmixed, just as the import of His saying وَمِزاجُهُ مِنْ تَسْنِيمٍ is that it is mixed into the cup of the righteous (الأبرار) containing the sealed pure wine. This indicates, firstly, that Tasnim is superior to the sealed pure wine, which increases in delight when mixed with it. Secondly, it indicates that those brought near are of a higher degree than the righteous described in the verses.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ أَجْرَمُوا كانُوا مِنَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا يَضْحَكُونَ "Indeed the guilty used to laugh at those who believed" (83:29). The context suggests that "those who believed" refers to the righteous described in the previous verses. They are referred to as "those who believed" because the reason for the disbelievers laughing at them and mocking them was their faith (الإيمان). Similarly, the disbelievers are referred to as "the guilty" (الذين أجرموا) to indicate that by doing so, they are among the criminals (المجرمين).

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذا مَرُّوا بِهِمْ يَتَغامَزُونَ "and when they passed them by, they would wink at each other" (83:30). This is conjoined to His saying يَضْحَكُونَ "they used to laugh". That is, when they passed by those who believed, they would wink at one another and signal with their eyes in mockery of them.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذَا انْقَلَبُوا إِلى أَهْلِهِمُ انْقَلَبُوا فَكِهِينَ "and when they returned to their folks, they would return jubilant" (83:31). Jubilant (الفكه) refers to being merry and boastful. The meaning is that when they returned and went back to their families after their laughing and winking, they returned delighted and joyful with what they had done. Or it is from fakahah (الفكاهة) in the sense of the conversation of intimate companions, meaning they returned talking about what they had done by way of amusement.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذا رَأَوْهُمْ قالُوا إِنَّ هؤُلاءِ لَضالُّونَ "and when they saw them, they would say, ‘Indeed these are the ones who have gone astray’" (83:32). This was by way of bearing witness against them of misguidance or passing judgment upon them; the latter is more likely.

His saying, the Exalted: وَما أُرْسِلُوا عَلَيْهِمْ حافِظِينَ "though they had not been sent as guardians over them" (83:33). That is, these who were guilty were not sent as guardians over the believers to judge them as they pleased or to bear witness against them according to their whims. This is a form of sarcasm (تهكم) directed at the mockers.

His saying, the Exalted: فَالْيَوْمَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مِنَ الْكُفَّارِ يَضْحَكُونَ "So today the believers laugh at the faithless" (83:34). "Today" refers to the Day of Recompense. Referring to "the guilty" as "the faithless" (الكفار) is a return to their true attribute. It is said that placing the prepositional phrase مِنَ الْكُفَّارِ "at the faithless" before the verb يَضْحَكُونَ "laugh" is to indicate an inversion of the situation (قصر القلب). The meaning is: Today, those who believe laugh at the faithless, not the faithless at them as they used to do in the world.

His saying, the Exalted: عَلَى الْأَرائِكِ يَنْظُرُونَ هَلْ ثُوِّبَ الْكُفَّارُ ما كانُوا يَفْعَلُونَ "on couches, watching. Have the faithless been rewarded for what they used to do?" (83:35-36). Reward (الثواب) originally refers to any recompense (الجزاء) in general, though its usage has become predominant for good. His saying عَلَى الْأَرائِكِ "on couches" is a predicate after a predicate for "those who believe," and يَنْظُرُونَ "watching" is another predicate. His saying هَلْ ثُوِّبَ "Have [they] been rewarded..." etc., is related to His saying يَنْظُرُونَ "watching", standing in the place of the object.

The meaning is: Those who believe are on raised beds in decorated chambers, looking at the recompense of the faithless for the deeds they used to do in the world—the various types of crimes, including their laughing at the believers, their winking at them when they passed by, their returning to their families jubilant, and their saying: "Indeed these are the ones who have gone astray."

(Narrative Discussion)

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَفِي ذلِكَ فَلْيَتَنافَسِ الْمُتَنافِسُونَ "for that let the aspirers aspire", he said: "In that which we have mentioned of the reward that the believer seeks."

In al-Majma', regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذا مَرُّوا بِهِمْ يَتَغامَزُونَ "and when they passed them by, they would wink at each other", it is said that it was revealed concerning Ali ibn Abi Talib (peace be upon him). This was because he was among a group of Muslims who came to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and the hypocrites mocked them, laughed, and winked at one another. Then they returned to their companions and said: "Today we saw the bald one (الأصلع) and laughed at him." Thus, the verse was revealed before Ali and his companions reached the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). This is narrated from Muqatil and al-Kalbi.

I say: It has also been cited in al-Kashshaf.

In the same source, it is mentioned that al-Hakim Abu al-Qasim al-Haskani, in the book Shawahid al-Tanzil li-Qawa'id al-Tafsil, narrated with his chain from Abu Salih from Ibn Abbas, who said: "إِنَّ الَّذِينَ أَجْرَمُوا (Indeed the guilty) are the hypocrites of Quraysh, and الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا (those who believed) are Ali ibn Abi Talib and his companions."

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, regarding: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ أَجْرَمُوا (Indeed the guilty) until His saying فَكِهِينَ (jubilant), he said: "They mock."
